headline: From NASA Labs to African Classrooms: One Engineer's Mission
meta_description: A former NASA roboticist left her Silicon Valley career to teach artificial intelligence to 1,000+ young people across four continents.
summary_short: An engineer who helped build GPS satellites now teaches AI to underserved communities worldwide, believing tech education is a right, not a privilege.
body:
At the peak of her career—holding one of the world's rarest technical positions as senior principal technologist in robotics AI at Amazon—an engineer made an unexpected choice: she walked away.

Ms. Jetter had spent two decades developing autonomous algorithms for spacecraft and robots, after studying at MIT and Stanford. But she became convinced that AI's future couldn't be shaped only in corporate labs and prestigious institutions.

"If we do not have all the right voices in the room shaping artificial intelligence," she said, "the product we end up with will not be the best one for us as a global community."

That conviction led her to create thinqueBytes, a non-profit breaking down complex subjects—AI, robotics, rocket science—into short educational videos answering fundamental questions. Will AI take my job? Should I be afraid of it? Since its launch, the program has trained more than 1,000 young people across four continents, including in Ghana.

Ms. Jetter's childhood—building circuits from neighbourhood scraps, her mother a special education teacher—shaped her belief that understanding emerging technology "should not be a privilege; it should be a right."

In July, she brought this vision to the UN's first Global Dialogue on AI Governance in Geneva, where Secretary-General Guterres called AI "the great equaliser of the twenty-first century"—if governed well and shared widely. For Ms. Jetter, that sharing begins in classrooms, not boardrooms.
